Elihu asks those present to decide with him about Job's words. Any Christian whose mind is enlightened, whose heart is made holy by the Spirit of God, and who understands the Scriptures can judge how far words or actions agree with true religion better than anyone who relies on their own understanding. Job had spoken as if he meant entirely to justify himself. Whoever says, “I have cleansed my hands in vain,” offends against God’s children and pleases his enemies, saying what they say.

Elihu showed Job that God did not mean him any harm by afflicting him, but intended it for his spiritual benefit. Here he shows that God did him no wrong by afflicting him. If this did not satisfy him, it ought to silence him. God cannot do evil, nor the Almighty commit wrongdoing. Even if services now go unrewarded and sins now go unpunished, there will be a day when God will fully reward every person according to his works. Further, even though the believer’s final condemnation is removed through the Savior's ransom, he has deserved worse than any outward affliction; so no wrong is done to him, however he may be tested.

Elihu appeals directly to Job himself. Could he suppose that God was like those earthly princes who hate what is right, who are unfit to rule and prove a scourge to mankind? It is presumptuous to condemn God’s actions, as Job had done through his discontent. Elihu suggests several considerations to Job to produce high thoughts of God and so persuade him to submit. Job had often wished to plead his case before God. Elihu asks, To what purpose? Everything that God does is right and will be found so. What can make those uneasy whose souls are at peace in God? The smiles of all the world cannot quiet those on whom God frowns.

When we correct someone for wrongdoing, we must also point to what is good. Job’s friends wanted him to acknowledge that he was a wicked man. It would be enough if he admitted that he spoke rashly with his lips. When giving correction, let us not make things worse than they are. Elihu directs Job to humble himself before God for his sins and accept the punishment. Also, to pray to God to reveal his sins to him. A good person is willing to know the worst about himself; especially under affliction, he wants to be told why God contends with him. It is not enough to be sorry for our sins, but we must go and sin no more. And if we are loving children, we will love to speak with our Father and tell him everything on our minds. Elihu reasons with Job about his discontent under affliction. We are ready to think that everything concerning us should be just as we want it; but it is not reasonable to expect this. Elihu asks whether there was sin and folly in what Job said. God is righteous in all his ways, and holy in all his works. The believer says, Let my Savior, my wise and loving Lord, choose everything for me. I am sure that will be wisest and best for his glory and my good.
